section.title: Nineveh Repents
section.sectionRationale: The word of the LORD comes a second time, Jonah finally obeys, and the whole great city, from king to cattle, turns from its evil, so God relents from the disaster. It is one complete unit of call, preaching, repentance, and mercy.
section.thesis: The word of the LORD comes again, a five-word sermon shakes a whole pagan city to repentance, and the God who threatened disaster relents in mercy.
section.frameNote: Three scenes: the second call and Jonah's obedience; the preaching and the city's repentance; the king's decree and God relenting.

verses.text: Then the word of the LORD came to Jonah a second time:
“Get up! Go to the great city of Nineveh and {{qara|proclaim}} to it the message that I give you.”
This time Jonah got up and went to Nineveh, in accordance with the word of the LORD. Now Nineveh was an exceedingly great city, requiring a three-day journey.

units.label: The preaching and the repentance
units.note: from the greatest to the least

verses.text: On the first day of his journey, Jonah set out into the city and proclaimed, “Forty more days and Nineveh will be {{haphak|overturned}}!”
And the Ninevites believed God. They proclaimed a fast and dressed in sackcloth, from the greatest of them to the least.
When word reached the king of Nineveh, he got up from his throne, took off his royal robe, covered himself with sackcloth, and sat in ashes.
Then he issued a proclamation in Nineveh: “By the decree of the king and his nobles: Let no man or beast, herd or flock, taste anything at all. They must not eat or drink.
Furthermore, let both man and beast be covered with sackcloth, and have everyone call out earnestly to God. Let each one {{shuv|turn}} from his {{raah|evil}} ways and from the violence in his hands.
Who knows? God may turn and {{nacham|relent}}; He may turn from His fierce anger, so that we will not perish.”
When God saw their actions, that they had turned from their evil ways, He {{nacham|relented}} from the disaster He had threatened to bring upon them.

qara.gloss: To call, proclaim, or cry out. The LORD calls Jonah to proclaim, Jonah proclaims to the city, the Ninevites proclaim a fast, and everyone is to call out to God. The one word ties together God's summons, the preached word, and the people's cry for mercy.

haphak.gloss: To overturn, overthrow, or turn about. The same verb destroyed Sodom, yet it can also mean to be transformed. Jonah says Nineveh will be overturned, and it is, not in ruin but in repentance; the threatened overthrow becomes an overturning of hearts.

shuv.gloss: To turn, return, or repent. The Ninevites turn from their evil ways, and they hope God may turn from His anger. It is the great Old Testament word for repentance, a turning of the whole life back toward God.

raah.gloss: Evil, wickedness, or disaster. It names the evil ways the city abandons and, in the same breath, the disaster God had threatened. The word binds together the sin, the judgment it deserves, and the mercy that turns it aside.

nacham.gloss: To relent, be moved to compassion, or change a course of action. God relents from the threatened disaster when the city turns. This is not a change in God's character but the outworking of His consistent mercy toward the repentant.

basic.context: God speaks to Jonah a second time, and this time the prophet obeys and walks into Nineveh, the huge capital of a cruel empire. His message is blunt: forty more days and the city is finished. Astonishingly, the whole city believes God, from the king down to the animals, and turns from its evil. So God relents from the disaster.
basic.hermeneutics: The chapter shows real repentance and real mercy. People ask how such a total, sudden turning could happen, and whether the fasting animals are literal, but the writer's aim is clear: the worst of enemies can repent, and God is ready to forgive. When it says God relented, it means His mercy met their turning; His character never changes.
basic.originalLanguages: The word overturned, haphak, is a pun: the same verb overthrew Sodom, yet it can also mean to be transformed, and Nineveh is overturned by repentance rather than ruin. Turn, shuv, is the great word for repentance, and relent, nacham, describes God being moved to spare them.
basic.typology: Jonah, back from a death-like ordeal, preaching to Gentiles who repent, foreshadows the risen Christ sending His gospel to the nations. The whole scene is a preview of the Great Commission.
basic.christ: Jesus said the men of Nineveh will rise at the judgment and condemn His generation, because they repented at Jonah's preaching, and something greater than Jonah is here. If a five-word sermon from a reluctant prophet moved a pagan city, how much more should we heed Christ Himself.
basic.god: We learn that God is patient and merciful, quick to forgive the truly repentant, and that His compassion reaches the very enemies His people fear. His threats of judgment are real, yet they are aimed at repentance, not destruction.
basic.crossReferences: Jeremiah 18 explains how God relents toward a nation that turns from evil. Matthew 12 has Jesus point to Nineveh's repentance. And Luke 15 shows the same joy in heaven over sinners who turn.
basic.oneStory: The repentance of Nineveh is one of the Old Testament's clearest previews of the gospel going to the nations. A prophet raised from the deep preaches, Gentiles repent, and God shows mercy, pointing straight to Christ risen and the church sent to all peoples.

context.literary: The second half of the book mirrors the first: the same call, but now obedience instead of flight. The scene builds from Jonah's terse sermon to the city's sweeping response, greatest to least, man and beast, climaxing in God relenting. The repetition of turn and evil binds the human repentance to the divine mercy.
context.historical: Nineveh was a principal city of Assyria, an empire notorious for cruelty, later the destroyer of the northern kingdom. A citywide fast in sackcloth, decreed by the king, fits ancient practices of national mourning and appeal to the gods in the face of threatened calamity.
context.canonical: Nineveh's repentance stands out sharply against Israel's frequent refusal to heed her prophets. It anticipates the wideness of God's mercy celebrated across Scripture and becomes, on Jesus' lips, a witness against those who reject a greater prophet.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To confront Israel with the spectacle of her worst enemy repenting at a mere five-word sermon, and to display a God so merciful that He relents from disaster the moment a people turn, pressing the reader toward both repentance and joy over the salvation of outsiders.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes Nineveh's repentance and God's relenting. The abiding truths are that God calls all people to turn from evil, that genuine repentance is met with mercy, and that no one is beyond the reach of grace.

debates.question: What does it mean that God relented from the disaster?

views.label: God's unchanging mercy meeting repentance
views.case: Scripture says God does not change and does not lie; His relenting is the consistent outworking of His stated character, which always spares the truly repentant, as Jeremiah 18:7-8 explains.

views.label: A real divine response in the narrative
views.case: The text speaks plainly of God seeing their turning and relenting, portraying a genuine, responsive relationship rather than a fixed script; the language is meant to be taken seriously.

debates.bearing: Both guard the same truth: God is not capricious. His threat was aimed at repentance, and His mercy is sure for those who turn, so the relenting reveals His heart rather than a change in His nature.

debates.question: How could a whole pagan city repent so suddenly and completely?

views.label: A sovereign work of God
views.case: The same God who appointed the fish moved the city; the astonishing scale is exactly the point, magnifying grace and rebuking Israel's hardness.

views.label: A historically plausible crisis response
views.case: A city under threat, perhaps shaken by omens or Jonah's own strange account, could plausibly humble itself in a national fast; the details fit ancient practice.

debates.bearing: Whether one stresses the miracle or the plausibility, the writer holds it up as real repentance that shames the covenant people and glorifies God's mercy to the nations.

study.originalLanguages: The key words turn the scene on repentance and mercy. Qara, to call or proclaim, links God's summons, Jonah's preaching, and the people's cry to God. Haphak, overturned, is a deliberate pun: the verb that overthrew Sodom, yet also meaning to be transformed, so the threatened overthrow becomes an overturning of hearts. Shuv is the great word for turning, repentance, used of the people turning from evil and their hope that God will turn from anger. Ra'ah names both their evil and the disaster it deserved, and nacham describes God relenting, moved to spare them.

typology.type: Jonah preaching after his death-like ordeal
typology.fulfillment: The risen Christ sending the gospel of repentance to all nations, Luke 24:46-47.

typology.type: A Gentile city repenting at the word
typology.fulfillment: The ingathering of the nations in the book of Acts, as pagans believe and turn to God.

typology.type: The threatened overthrow turned to salvation
typology.fulfillment: Judgment borne and mercy given in Christ, so that those under sentence are spared.

study.christ: Jesus points to this very scene: the men of Nineveh will stand up at the judgment with this generation and condemn it, for they repented at the preaching of Jonah, and now One greater than Jonah is here, Matthew 12:41; Luke 11:32. The logic is piercing: if a reluctant prophet's brief warning turned a pagan city, how much more should people heed the risen Christ. Jonah's ministry to Gentiles after his deliverance foreshadows the gospel of repentance preached in Christ's name to all nations.
study.god: The LORD is patient, giving Nineveh forty days and a prophet. He is merciful, relenting from disaster the moment the city turns, showing that His threats aim at repentance. He is sovereign, able to move a whole hostile city to its knees. And His compassion is wide, embracing the very enemies His prophet despised, for He is God of the nations, not of Israel only.

crossReferences.note: If a nation turns from its evil, God relents from the disaster He planned, the principle behind Jonah 3:10.
The men of Nineveh repented at Jonah's preaching and will condemn those who reject One greater than Jonah.
Jesus again holds up Nineveh's repentance as a witness against unbelief.
Return to God with fasting, for He is gracious and may relent, the same pattern of turning and mercy.

study.oneStory: Nineveh's repentance is a vivid preview of the gospel going to the nations. A prophet brought up from the deep preaches, and Gentiles the covenant people feared turn to God and are spared. It points forward to the risen Christ, greater than Jonah, whose call to repentance goes out to every people, and to the joy of heaven over enemies made into worshippers.

sermon.title: The Obedient Prophet
sermon.why: From Begg's expository series A Study in Jonah, this message covers Jonah 3:1-10, Jonah's renewed commission and Nineveh's repentance, unfolding God's mercy to a pagan city and the breadth of His grace. A clear, orthodox exposition of this passage.

quiz.q: What was Jonah's message to Nineveh?

options.text: A stark warning: forty more days and Nineveh will be overturned.
options.note: Jonah 3:4 records a brief, blunt announcement of coming judgment.

options.text: A detailed call to worship the God of Israel.
options.note: The sermon was far shorter and sharper than that, a bare warning of overthrow.

options.text: A promise that the city would prosper.
options.note: The message threatened disaster, not prosperity.

options.text: A message he made up on his own.
options.note: It was the message the LORD gave him to proclaim.

quiz.q: How did the city respond to the preaching?

options.text: They believed God and repented, from the greatest to the least, in fasting and sackcloth.
options.note: Jonah 3:5-6 shows a sweeping, citywide turning, even the king.

options.text: Only a handful of people took it seriously.
options.note: The response was total, from king to commoner, not a handful.

options.text: They mocked Jonah and ignored the warning.
options.note: This is what we might expect, but the opposite happened.

options.text: They attacked Jonah for his message.
options.note: Far from attacking him, they humbled themselves before God.

quiz.q: What does it mean that God relented from the disaster?

options.text: His unchanging mercy met their repentance; the threat had always aimed at their turning.
options.note: God's relenting reveals His consistent character, not a change in His nature, Jeremiah 18:7-8.

options.text: God changed His mind and became a different God.
options.note: The relenting is real, but it is His steady mercy at work, not a change in who He is.

options.text: God had never really intended to judge them.
options.note: The threat was genuine; the mercy came in response to genuine repentance.

options.text: God was forced to back down against His will.
options.note: Nothing forces God; He freely shows the mercy that is His nature.

quiz.q: How does Jesus use the repentance of Nineveh?

options.text: As a witness against unbelief: they repented at Jonah, yet One greater than Jonah is here.
options.note: Matthew 12:41 and Luke 11:32 hold up Nineveh to shame those who reject Christ.

options.text: As proof that preaching is usually ineffective.
options.note: The opposite: it shows preaching bearing astonishing fruit.

options.text: As an example of a city God finally destroyed.
options.note: Here God spared the city; this misses the point of mercy.

options.text: Jesus makes no use of Nineveh's repentance.
options.note: Jesus directly appeals to it in the Gospels.

quiz.q: What is the wordplay in Nineveh will be overturned?

options.text: The verb can mean both destroyed and transformed; the city is overturned by repentance, not ruin.
options.note: Haphak carries both senses, so the threatened overthrow becomes an overturning of hearts.

options.text: It simply means the city walls would collapse.
options.note: The literal sense of destruction is there, but the pun points to transformation too.

options.text: It refers to a change of government only.
options.note: The turning is a repentance of the whole city before God, deeper than politics.

options.text: There is no wordplay; it means nothing beyond destruction.
options.note: The double meaning is deliberate and central to the scene.

questions: God gave Jonah a second call after he had failed the first. Where do you need to hear that God's mercy includes second chances for those who have run from Him?
A whole pagan city repented at a five-word sermon, while God's own people often would not listen. Is there hardness in your heart toward God's word that even outsiders might put to shame?
The king rose from his throne and sat in ashes. What would genuine humbling before God look like in your life, not just in feeling but in action?
Jesus said One greater than Jonah is here. If Nineveh turned at Jonah's warning, how are you responding to the far greater voice of Christ?
God's mercy reached the very enemies Jonah feared. Whose repentance would surprise or even trouble you, and how does God's heart for them reshape your prayers?

translationNotes.note: An exceedingly great city, requiring a three-day journey, may describe its size or, as the BSB footnote notes, be rendered a great city to God; the phrase stresses Nineveh's vastness.
Overturned translates haphak, the verb used of Sodom's overthrow, which can also mean transformed; the term carries a deliberate double sense.
Relented translates nacham; it describes God being moved to spare, in keeping with His revealed mercy toward the repentant, not a change in His character.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Jonah 3:1-10. The em dashes BSB prints in verse 10 have been rendered as commas per house style.
